items.q: What does Income Elasticity of Demand (YED) tell us?
items.correct: The sign tells us the good type: positive YED = normal good (rises with income); negative YED = inferior good (falls with income). YED > 1 indicates a luxury; YED between 0 and 1 a necessity.
items.misconception: A negative YED means the demand calculation went wrong.
items.random_wrong: YED measures how the rich and poor differ in tastes.
items.why: The negative sign for inferior goods (like own-brand pasta or bus travel) is meaningful, not an error. Students often treat negative elasticities as mistakes, missing what the sign signals about good type.

items.q: What is the difference between monetary and fiscal policy?
items.correct: Monetary policy uses interest rates and money supply controlled by the central bank; fiscal policy uses government taxation and spending controlled by the Treasury.
items.misconception: Monetary policy is about money and fiscal policy is about taxes.
items.random_wrong: Both terms mean the same thing — government economic policy.
items.why: While the rough distinction has some truth, the key analytical point is WHO controls each (independent central bank vs elected government) — central to debates on policy independence.

items.correct: The difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay — represented by the area below the demand curve and above the price line.
items.misconception: Consumer surplus is leftover stock that consumers haven't bought yet.
items.random_wrong: Consumer surplus is the profit that consumers make.
items.why: 'Surplus' is misleadingly physical-sounding. It is a welfare measure — the value consumers get beyond what they paid. Students take the everyday meaning of 'surplus' and miss the economic concept.

items.q: Which measure includes only those receiving unemployment benefits?
items.why: It's Claimant count. “ILO measure” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A part-time worker wanting more hours is best described as?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Underemployed. “Unemployed” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
